speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever come to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I assume you mean after I came. And yes, my mother came by herself in, I think, 1960, and visited me, visited the friends where I stayed in the beginning and some other-- ex-boyfriend from way back when, and his mother and various people. We still had some relatives in New York then-- second or third cousins of my mother's, not of mine. She came again, I think, by herself a couple of years later. And then both parents came after I had already finished my education here and was teaching. I normally went home in summers to see my parents. But that summer, they came to see me. Because I had just gotten the job at Pasadena City College and needed to prepare a course.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a teacher of French and French civilization. And since I have a--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents never moved here, they just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they came to visit?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to visit them, of course?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Did you ever marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never married. I also have no children.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 20th century, that generally-- one followed the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But some-- the question is, which order?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you became a teacher. And how long did you teach here?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21 years-- I taught as a teaching assistant at UCLA, where I studied. Got my BA and my MA in French with a German minor. Nobody, as far as I could see, wanted to learn Dutch. There was no sense in having a minor in that. And they have many more people that can teach better English than I can. There was no sense in that. So that left French and German. And I taught at UCLA for three years as a teaching assistant, and then got a permanent job at Pasadena City College and taught there for 18 years-- mostly French, but eventually also, Spanish, and towards the end, a couple of classes in German, and one time as a substitute for a friend-- Italian, beginning Italian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get connected with any other survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning, no, not ever. First of all, I didn't think that any other children had survived. I really thought I was the only one, because I had never met any. I had had had what I call cousins in Holland. And I knew they had survived. But we had also lost touch. I only knew that they had moved, also, to the States. I didn't know where they were. We have since found each other again. But other-- they are both older than I am. My age-- I did not know anybody-- any child that had survived. So I didn't know there were any others.

speaker: interviewee
text: As for the adults, mostly, if I knew that somebody was a survivor, I mostly avoided them, even if only because they would say, well, you were just a child. What do you know anyway?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were much too young. You don't remember anything. Your memories are all wrong. So-- and I didn't really see where I would need to go through that. At the very first time that I had any kind of a problem, really, was because nobody had told me-- I was still at UCLA. I was still a student. So this was in the-- I came in '59. It was in the early '60s, 15 years and more after the war. Nobody had told me that 10 o'clock in the morning on the last Friday-- or the first Friday, whenever it was of the month-- the sirens went off. And off they went. And my instinctive reaction was to run as fast as I could to the only safe place I knew. And that was the restrooms in the library, because they were underground. And I sat there, shivering and shaking, for about 15 minutes, trying to talk myself out of this particular mood and telling myself that no, the war has been over. And this is not what you think it is. And then for the longest time again, I had no problems at all. I also didn't meet anybody else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't actually meet child survivors and more adults until the mid '80s, when I began, after my retirement, to have problems with flashbacks and nightmares and so on, none of which had happened before, and looked for help. It was a bit difficult in finding. But I did find it. It fell in my lap, actually. And in the process, found the child survivors, a group of child survivors. And for the first time, found out that no, I wasn't the only one, and there were plenty of us. And they're all nice people. And so I acquired, not only a number of friends, but a number of brothers and sisters, because that's what it feels like. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of the child survivor's sisters sent me a note the other day that she made on the computer. And it says, child survivors are the family we choose for ourselves. And I framed that and put it on my desk. Because it's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have done a great deal of writing. What have you been writing about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lately, mostly the war years, and the camp years, and the whole Holocaust experience-- I have always written my whole life. When I was very young, I told myself stories. Once I had learned how to write, I wrote them. Those which are still extant date from my high school days. And even then, they deal a lot with being alone, being isolated, being different. That was post-war. Nowadays, the themes are still there, but in a little different way. I write poetry describing the camps, sometimes dreams of the camps-- that's happened as well-- reactions to things that remind me of Holocaust days or whatever comes up. In prose, there have been several pieces on anger and fear. There also has been, in prose, a second book, which will come out in June, called And that's, again, it's my story, but written in prose, whereas the poems have been collected in a volume, And it's essentially a Holocaust memoir, even though there are some other poems in there as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you've had them published, or you've had published.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the other one is being published.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what libraries would have that particular book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, offhand, I don't know which libraries bought the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you sent it to anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it is being sent, even as we speak. It's going to the Pomona Library and the Claremont Library. Pomona is independent. Claremont belongs to the LA County library system. And it goes to 100 other libraries in and around the country. The-- again, the Martyrs' Memorial has it, so does the Wiesenthal, so does the Washington Museu, so does Scheveningen [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in that sense, it's going around. And a number of child survivors have it and have recommended it to their synagogues or collections or whatever. So it is in other places as well, though I don't necessarily know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: What has writing-- this writing done for you emotionally?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's doing several things. It's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's not therapeutic in the sense that I can then forget about it, because one doesn't. But it is in the sense that the thoughts and the images get onto paper. And I can look at it. And sometimes, at the end of a poem I say, hmm, I didn't know I was going to say that. But that's true. And so in that sense, things come out. It feels very often as those things don't come out of me, but through me. Because very often, there are things that are, apparently, buried so deeply that I can't recall them at will. But they come out in the poems.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it, it does several things, in addition to which, of course, it, it shows other people what it was like. I use them in lecturing at schools. If I have enough time, I lecture and intersperse with poetry. Is there something you haven't said that you'd like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say to people a few hundred years from now who would hear your story? And what would you-- what would you tell them about that time during the war? What would you like to impart?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's hard to answer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think one of the things, apart from the horrors of war-- for which people don't need me. All you have to look is look at Bosnia. Apart from that, I think one of the most important things that did not happen to me and that should have happened to all children, is a lot more explanation and a lot more telling by adults, parents or otherwise. We are going here for such and such a reason. We are doing this for so such and such a reason. You must not say so and so because, and so on, instead of just saying, do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to show your pictures now and have you explain them to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. That is a picture of me at aged five, 1938. And it was taken in Berlin, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sorry about that. My name is Gabriele Silten. That is my paternal grandfather, Dr. Ernst Silten, aged, then 76 in 1942, in Berlin, Germany, in his office. That is the last picture that was taken before his death-- about six months before his death. That is a picture of me, Gabriele Silten, with my paternal grandmother, Marta Silten. My grandmother is 58 years old. I am two. It must have been 1935, also taken in Berlin, Germany. That is my grandfather, Ernst Silten's pharmacy, called the Kaiser Friedrich Apotheke-- the Emperor Frederick Pharmacy-- which was located in Berlin, Germany, which he owned and operated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it his or his, his father's before him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was his. My grandfather bought it. And my father eventually co-owned it. That is my maternal grandfather, Richard Teppich It's taken at least near Berlin, Germany. He' s always photographed with his yachting cap. He didn't really have a yacht, but he had a small boat. And he adored his yachting cap and boating. So he's always taken-- his photographs always show that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I think he must be in his 60s, but I'm not sure. That is my maternal grandmother, Gertrude Teppich, whom I knew as Omi Trudel. She's 58 years old. It's 1938. And it was taken in Berlin, Germany. That is my father Dr. Fritz Silten. It's taken in Berlin, Germany, in 1935. He was 31 years old. And I was already on the scene. That is my mother, Ilse Silten, age there, 26. It's 1935. And that was also taken in Berlin, Germany. That is a picture of me, Gabriele Silten, aged eight in 1941. And that was taken already in Holland-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Amsterdam, Holland, actually, to be exact. That is another picture of me, Gabriele Silten, taken directly on our return from Theresienstadt in 1945. As we arrived at the central station in Amsterdam, ID pictures were taken. And that's it. I'm 12 years old. That is my friend, my school friend, Max, the way I knew him and the way he looked directly after the war. That picture was taken in 1947. And he's 12 or 13. He and I sat on the same school bench in first grade and had all sorts of adventures together. And that's why he's here. It's taken in Amsterdam, Holland. And that's me again, Gabriele, in '47. And I'm 14. It's taken at Amsterdam, Holland. And it's one of a series that was taken when a photographer came to the house. What you see is a little, very formal kind of a poem, as were written when I was a child in what we then called poetry albums. And your friends and parents and so on wrote their little poems, pretty much always the same, and decorated the page or both pages with the kind of pictures you see there. I got this for my eighth birthday. And this particular piece is written by Carla, the little girl that lived-- that was my upstairs neighbors before and after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back to the same apartment after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As it happened, the woman that had had the apartment while we were gone was a Dutch Nazi sympathizer. And so the, the people above us immediately denounced her to the police soon as the war was over. And she was arrested almost immediately. And the apartment came empty. And so we got it back. So it was a rented place, of course. That is the bear that was a present to me on my very first birthday. And I've had that bear ever since, actually, minus a very short time-- a couple of years or so-- that she was in hiding with my neighbor, Carla. She looks a little the worse for wear. Because when I was a child, I practiced hair cutting on her, not knowing that their hair wouldn't grow back. And over the years, the soles of her feet and the palms of her hands had acquired a number of holes. And the straw came out, slowly but surely. So they have been repaired. Other than that, she is the original. And as mentioned before, the outfit is a copy of the original. Because the original is rotting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this has been your closest friend for many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this has been my closest friend for many years, and to a great extent, still is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Brunette. In the Dutch version of Goldilocks, the papa bear is called Bruno, and the mama bear is called Bruna, and the baby bear is called Brunette. So my bear was called Brunette after the baby bear.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also have a doll collection, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do, a rather a big one by now-- I don't know, in the neighborhood of probably about 70 or 80 by now. So they're kind of running me out of my room, because there is really no room to put anymore. They have to be pretty special before I add anymore now. As I had dolls when I was a child, so it's not, you know, kind of keeping track of my childhood. But I've always liked dolls. And I still like dolls. So nowadays, I have a doll collection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Gabriele.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome, Dana.
